---
output:
  pdf_document:
    toc: true
    number_sections: true
    latex_engine: xelatex
  html_document:
    toc: true
    df_print: paged
---
```{r}
###############################################################################
# Project: EA Macroinvertebrates – Data Access & Inspection 
# Purpose: (i) Load required packages, (ii) download EA Open Data parquet files,
#          (iii) open them as Arrow Datasets (no in-memory materialization),
#          (iv) sanity-check schemas and key fields.
#
# Inputs   : Remote parquet files hosted at environment.data.gov.uk (URLs below)
# Outputs  : Local copies in the working directory:
#              - R_INV_WHPT_METRICS_B.parquet
#              - INV_OPEN_DATA_SITE.parquet
#              - INV_OPEN_DATA_METRICS.parquet
#              - INV_OPEN_DATA_TAXA.parquet
#
# Notes    :
#   • We *do not* mutate or re-save raw files here – this script is read-only
#     apart from first-time downloads. Subsequent cleaning happens in later chunks.
#   • Arrow Datasets let us query with dplyr verbs and delay `collect()` until
#     needed – essential for performance and reproducibility at scale.
#   • Keep this chunk minimal and deterministic; heavy joins/filters belong later.
#
# Dependencies loaded here (used now and in later chunks):
#   - Core data:     arrow, dplyr, tidyr, lubridate, magrittr
#   - Modelling:     mgcv
#   - I/O:           downloader
#   - Plotting:      ggplot2, scales
#   - Mapping:       sf, rnaturalearth, rnaturalearthdata, units
#   - Utilities:     stringr, purrr, janitor, tibble
#
# Repro tips:
#   • Consider pinning versions with `renv::init()` in the project root.
#   • If repeatedly running, you may wrap `download.file()` calls with file.exists()
#     guards in a later iteration; we leave your original calls unchanged here.
###############################################################################

# Always begin in the project root 
setwd("~/Desktop/FRESHWATER MICROINVERTEBRATE")

# -----------------------------
# STEP 1: LOAD REQUIRED LIBRARIES
# -----------------------------
# Core
library(arrow)       # Fast columnar access; Arrow Datasets via dplyr
library(dplyr)       # Grammar of data manipulation
library(ggplot2)     # Plotting (EDA / quick checks)
library(lubridate)   # Date parsing and year/month helpers
library(tidyr)       # NA handling, reshaping
library(mgcv)        # GAM / GAMM modelling
library(downloader)  # Robust downloading (binary mode)
library(magrittr)    # Pipe-friendly tools (%>% and friends)
library(gratia)      # Tools for GAMs: smooth checks, diagnostics, plots

# Used later in the analysis 
library(sf)                 # Simple features: projections, geometry ops (maps)
library(rnaturalearth)      # Natural Earth basemaps (countries/regions)
library(rnaturalearthdata)  # Data package for rnaturalearth
library(units)              # Units support (sf coordinate conversions)
library(scales)             # Axis formatting (log breaks, percent, etc.)
library(stringr)            # String helpers for consistent naming
library(purrr)              # Functional maps over families / lists
library(janitor)            # clean_names(), tabyl() for quick QA
library(tibble)             # tibble(), enframe() (nice printing)
library(forcats)            # Factor handling: reorder, recode, lump levels
library(grid)               # Low-level grid graphics (useful for annotation / layout)
library(rlang)              # Tidy evaluation tools for programming with dplyr/ggplot2
library(MASS)               # Statistical functions/models (e.g., distributions)

# Keep Arrow’s `pull()` behaviour stable (suppresses the deprecation warning)
# This does NOT change results; it only avoids noise in the console.
options(arrow.pull_as_vector = TRUE)

# -----------------------------
# STEP 2: DOWNLOAD EA PARQUET DATA (only once)
# -----------------------------
# These are authoritative open-data endpoints. We keep your original calls,
# which always (re)download; in production, add `if (!file.exists())` guards.
download.file("https://environment.data.gov.uk/ecology/explorer/downloads/R_INV_WHPT_METRICS_B.parquet",
              destfile = "R_INV_WHPT_METRICS_B.parquet", mode = "wb")

download.file("https://environment.data.gov.uk/ecology/explorer/downloads/INV_OPEN_DATA_SITE.parquet",
              destfile = "INV_OPEN_DATA_SITE.parquet", mode = "wb")

download.file("https://environment.data.gov.uk/ecology/explorer/downloads/INV_OPEN_DATA_METRICS.parquet",
              destfile = "INV_OPEN_DATA_METRICS.parquet", mode = "wb")

download.file("https://environment.data.gov.uk/ecology/explorer/downloads/INV_OPEN_DATA_TAXA.parquet",
              destfile = "INV_OPEN_DATA_TAXA.parquet", mode = "wb")

# -----------------------------
# STEP 3: OPEN DATASETS USING ARROW (DO NOT COLLECT YET)
# -----------------------------
# Virtual pointers (Arrow Datasets). We operate lazily via dplyr; call `collect()`
# only when you truly need an in-memory R data.frame/tibble.
sites_dataset   <- open_dataset("INV_OPEN_DATA_SITE.parquet")
metrics_dataset <- open_dataset("INV_OPEN_DATA_METRICS.parquet")
taxa_dataset    <- open_dataset("INV_OPEN_DATA_TAXA.parquet")
whpt_dataset    <- open_dataset("R_INV_WHPT_METRICS_B.parquet")

# -----------------------------
# STEP 4: QUICK INSPECTION (UNCOMMENT TO VIEW)
# -----------------------------
# `glimpse()` on Arrow Datasets shows schema and example values without pulling all data.
# For a concrete peek at rows, do: `metrics_dataset %>% head(5) %>% collect()`
glimpse(sites_dataset)
glimpse(metrics_dataset)
glimpse(taxa_dataset)
glimpse(whpt_dataset)

# Stratify by analysis method to understand coverage and heterogeneity.
# We end with `collect()` to bring back a small summary table only.
metrics_dataset %>%
  group_by(ANALYSIS_METHOD, ANALYSIS_METHOD_DESCRIPTION) %>%
  summarise(n = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(desc(n)) %>%
  collect()

```

```{r}
###############################################################################
# Project: EA Macroinvertebrates – Data Cleaning & Preprocessing 
# Purpose: Build analysis-ready datasets per focal family, implementing the
#          rules for categorical vs numerical abundances,
#          1-significant-figure rounding, bounds, and replicate handling.
#
# Key logic:
#   • ANALYSIS_METHOD
#        - ANLE: treat as categorical (bins)
#        - ANAA: treat as numerical (counts; round to 1 s.f. if needed)
#        - ANLA: meaning changed over time → decide per-sample using TAXA table
#               (presence of placeholders 3/33/333/3333 ⇒ categorical),
#               else fall back to year threshold (pre-2000 ≈ categorical)
#   • Categorical bins:
#        - 1 is its own class (exact 1)
#        - 3 ≈ 2–9, 33 ≈ 10–99, 333 ≈ 100–999, 3333 ≈ 1000–9999
#   • Numerical counts:
#        - Accurate to ~1 s.f.; for x ≥ 10, treat as rounded to 1 s.f.
#        - Use half-up rounding consistent with EA practice (5s round up)
#        - Convert 1 s.f. values to lower/upper bounds (e.g., 20 → 15–24)
#   • Sampling method:
#        - Prefer S3PO only (recommended), but keep switchable with FILTER_S3PO
#   • Replicates:
#        - Keep first sample per (SITE_ID, SAMPLE_DATE), drop later same-day reps
#
# Output objects created by this chunk:
#   • data_by_family: named list of data.frames (one per focal family)
#   • data_all_families: single combined data.frame across focal families
#
# Notes:
#   - This chunk deliberately avoids modelling; it only prepares clean inputs.
#   - Heavy lifting uses Arrow laziness; we `collect()` only when needed.
###############################################################################

# --- Utility: silence messages/warnings from this chunk without changing results ---
silent <- function(expr) {
  .nul <- if (.Platform$OS.type == "windows") "NUL" else "/dev/null"
  oldw <- getOption("warn"); options(warn = -1)
  on.exit(options(warn = oldw), add = TRUE)

  # capture messages (stderr)
  invisible(capture.output(
    {
      # capture regular output (stdout)
      invisible(capture.output(
        {
          suppressPackageStartupMessages(
            suppressMessages(
              suppressWarnings(
                force(expr)
              )
            )
          )
        },
        file = .nul, type = "output"
      ))
    },
    file = .nul, type = "message"
  ))
}

silent({
  # ==================================================================
  # DATA CLEANING & PREPROCESSING — ALL FIVE FAMILIES
  # Implements rules for categorical vs numerical
  # ==================================================================

  # ---------- PARAMETERS ----------
  # Focal families and global switches. MIN_YEAR trims very early samples.
  FAMILIES   <- c("Aphelocheiridae","Brachycentridae","Cordulegastridae",
                  "Odontoceridae","Potamanthidae")
  MIN_YEAR   <- 1990
  FILTER_S3PO <- TRUE  # recommended by EA

  # ---------- OPEN DATASETS ----------
  # Arrow Datasets: lazy, columnar access; we postpone collect() to when needed.
  metrics_ds <- open_dataset("INV_OPEN_DATA_METRICS.parquet")
  sites_ds   <- open_dataset("INV_OPEN_DATA_SITE.parquet")
  taxa_ds    <- open_dataset("INV_OPEN_DATA_TAXA.parquet")
  whpt_ds    <- open_dataset("R_INV_WHPT_METRICS_B.parquet")

  # ---------- 1) METRICS + SITES (do as much in Arrow as possible) ----------
  # • Filter to analysis methods of interest (ANAA/ANLA/ANLE)
  # • Optionally restrict to S3PO sampling method (recommended)
  # • Cast IDs to consistent int32 for reliable joins
  # • Attach site metadata (EA area, catchment, easting/northing)
  # • Drop same-day replicate samples (keep first by sort order)
  # • Bring to R and add date fields + factors
  metrics_clean <- metrics_ds %>%
    filter(ANALYSIS_METHOD %in% c("ANAA","ANLA","ANLE")) %>%
    { if (FILTER_S3PO) filter(., SAMPLE_METHOD == "S3PO") else . } %>%
    dplyr::mutate(SITE_ID   = cast(SITE_ID,   int32()),
           SAMPLE_ID = cast(SAMPLE_ID, int32())) %>%
    dplyr::select(SITE_ID, SAMPLE_ID, SAMPLE_DATE, SAMPLE_METHOD, ANALYSIS_TYPE, ANALYSIS_METHOD) %>%
    dplyr::left_join(
      sites_ds %>%
        dplyr::mutate(SITE_ID = cast(SITE_ID, int32())) %>%
        dplyr::select(SITE_ID, REPORTING_AREA, CATCHMENT, WATER_BODY, FULL_EASTING, FULL_NORTHING),
      by = "SITE_ID"
    ) %>%
    dplyr::arrange(SITE_ID, SAMPLE_DATE, SAMPLE_ID) %>%
    dplyr::distinct(SITE_ID, SAMPLE_DATE, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%  # remove same-day duplicates
    dplyr::collect() %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      SAMPLE_DATE = as.Date(SAMPLE_DATE),
      Year        = year(SAMPLE_DATE),
      decimal_date = decimal_date(SAMPLE_DATE),
      SITE_F      = factor(SITE_ID)
    ) %>%
    dplyr::filter(Year >= MIN_YEAR)

  # Keep the SAMPLE_ID universe we’ll work with
  sample_ids <- unique(metrics_clean$SAMPLE_ID)

  # ---------- 2) TAXA-DERIVED SAMPLE FLAGS (Q&A rules) ----------
  # Decide if each sample is *categorical* or *numerical* by inspecting TAXA rows:
  #  - Presence of placeholders (3,33,333,...) ⇒ categorical
  #  - ANLA needs this per-sample decision; ANAA/ANLE handled below
  cat_placeholders <- c(3, 33, 333, 3333, 33333, 333333)

  taxa_flags <- taxa_ds %>%
    dplyr::filter(SAMPLE_ID %in% sample_ids) %>%
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(TOTAL_ABUNDANCE) & TOTAL_ABUNDANCE > 0) %>%
    dplyr::select(SAMPLE_ID, TOTAL_ABUNDANCE) %>%
    collect() %>%
    mutate(
      is_placeholder = TOTAL_ABUNDANCE %in% cat_placeholders,
      is_one         = TOTAL_ABUNDANCE == 1
    ) %>%
    group_by(SAMPLE_ID) %>%
    summarise(
      any_placeholder = any(is_placeholder),            # any 3/33/333 present?
      all_in_catset   = all(is_placeholder | is_one),   # only 1 or placeholders?
      has_one         = any(is_one),                    # at least one exact "1"?
      .groups = "drop"
    ) %>%
    mutate(
      # If any placeholder or the whole set is categorical-style → categorical
      abundance_type_taxa = ifelse(any_placeholder | all_in_catset, "categorical", "numerical")
    )

  # Merge TAXA-derived flag with METRICS, resolve ANLA ambiguity with date fallback.
  # Also carry a flag indicating whether the categorical set includes exact "1".
  samples_universe <- metrics_clean %>%
    dplyr::left_join(taxa_flags, by = "SAMPLE_ID") %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      abundance_type = case_when(
        ANALYSIS_METHOD == "ANLE" ~ "categorical",
        ANALYSIS_METHOD == "ANAA" ~ "numerical",
        ANALYSIS_METHOD == "ANLA" & !is.na(abundance_type_taxa) ~ abundance_type_taxa,
        ANALYSIS_METHOD == "ANLA" &  is.na(abundance_type_taxa) & Year <  2000 ~ "categorical",
        ANALYSIS_METHOD == "ANLA" &  is.na(abundance_type_taxa) & Year >= 2000 ~ "numerical",
        TRUE ~ abundance_type_taxa
      ),
      cat_has_one = ifelse(abundance_type == "categorical" & !is.na(has_one), has_one, FALSE)
    ) %>%
    dplyr::select(-any_placeholder, -all_in_catset, -abundance_type_taxa, -has_one)

  # ---------- 3) WHPT FAMILY DATA ----------
  # Extract family-level totals for focal families; keep only our SAMPLE_ID universe.
  whpt_df <- whpt_ds %>%
    dplyr::mutate(SITE_ID = cast(SITE_ID, int32()),
           SAMPLE_ID = cast(SAMPLE_ID, int32())) %>%
    dplyr::filter(EQ_TAXON_UNIT %in% FAMILIES, SAMPLE_ID %in% sample_ids) %>%
    dplyr::select(SITE_ID, SAMPLE_ID, EQ_TAXON_UNIT, TOTAL_NUMBER) %>%
    dplyr::collect()

  # ---------- 4) NUMERICAL 1-SF (HALF-UP) BOUNDS ----------
  # Rounding helper consistent with EA practice (5s round up).
  round1sf_half_up <- function(x) {
    ifelse(x == 0, 0, {
      d <- floor(log10(abs(x)))
      m <- x / 10^d
      m_rounded <- floor(m * 10 + 0.5) / 10
      m_rounded * 10^d
    })
  }
  # Convert 1-s.f. value to closed interval on the original integer scale.
  # e.g., 20 (1 s.f.) → [15, 24]; 100 → [95, 149]; 7 → [7, 7]
  numeric_bounds_1sf <- function(v) {
    out_lower <- numeric(length(v))
    out_upper <- numeric(length(v))
    for (i in seq_along(v)) {
      x <- v[i]
      if (is.na(x) || x <= 0) { out_lower[i] <- 0; out_upper[i] <- 0; next }
      if (x <= 9) { out_lower[i] <- x; out_upper[i] <- x; next }
      d   <- floor(log10(x))
      base <- 10^d
      rep <- round1sf_half_up(x)
      m   <- as.integer(round(rep / base))
      lo  <- (m - 0.5) * base
      hi  <- (m + 0.5) * base - 1
      out_lower[i] <- floor(lo + 1e-9)
      out_upper[i] <- floor(hi + 1e-9)
    }
    data.frame(lower = out_lower, upper = out_upper)
  }

  # ---------- 5) BUILD PER-FAMILY DATASETS ----------
  # For each family:
  #  • Build the sampling frame from METRICS (sites where family was ever recorded)
  #  • Left-join WHPT totals for that family; fill absences with zero
  #  • Assign categorical vs numerical bounds following the rules
  build_family_df <- function(fam) {
    # Sites where the family appears at least once (ensures sensible universe)
    sites_with_fam <- whpt_df %>%
      dplyr::filter(EQ_TAXON_UNIT == fam) %>%
      dplyr::distinct(SITE_ID) %>%
      dplyr::pull(SITE_ID)
    if (!length(sites_with_fam)) {
      warning(sprintf("No records for %s in WHPT data.", fam))
      return(NULL)
    }

    # Candidate samples at those sites (with metadata + method flags)
    fam_samples <- samples_universe %>%
      dplyr::filter(SITE_ID %in% sites_with_fam) %>%
      dplyr::select(SITE_ID, SAMPLE_ID, SAMPLE_DATE, Year, decimal_date, SITE_F,
             REPORTING_AREA, CATCHMENT, WATER_BODY, FULL_EASTING, FULL_NORTHING,
             ANALYSIS_METHOD, abundance_type, cat_has_one)

    # Family totals for those samples (may be missing → later filled to zero)
    fam_records <- whpt_df %>%
      dplyr::filter(EQ_TAXON_UNIT == fam) %>%
      dplyr::select(SITE_ID, SAMPLE_ID, TOTAL_NUMBER)

    # Join and derive presence flag
    df <- fam_samples %>%
      dplyr::left_join(fam_records, by = c("SITE_ID","SAMPLE_ID")) %>%
      dplyr::mutate(
        TOTAL_NUMBER = replace_na(TOTAL_NUMBER, 0L),
        present      = TOTAL_NUMBER > 0L
      )

    # In categorical sets that include an exact 1, interpret “3” as 2–9 (not 1–9)
    cat_lower <- ifelse(df$cat_has_one, 2L, 1L)

    # Preallocate lower/upper and populate by case
    lower <- upper <- rep(NA_real_, nrow(df))

    # Absences → [0,0]
    idx0 <- which(!df$present)
    if (length(idx0)) { lower[idx0] <- 0; upper[idx0] <- 0 }

    # Categorical bins (1, 3, 33, 333, 3333)
    idx_cat <- which(df$present & df$abundance_type == "categorical")
    if (length(idx_cat)) {
      tn <- df$TOTAL_NUMBER[idx_cat]
      lower[idx_cat] <- dplyr::case_when(
        tn == 1L    ~ 1,
        tn == 3L    ~ cat_lower[idx_cat],
        tn == 33L   ~ 10,
        tn == 333L  ~ 100,
        tn == 3333L ~ 1000,
        TRUE        ~ NA_real_
      )
      upper[idx_cat] <- dplyr::case_when(
        tn == 1L    ~ 1,
        tn == 3L    ~ 9,
        tn == 33L   ~ 99,
        tn == 333L  ~ 999,
        tn == 3333L ~ 9999,
        TRUE        ~ NA_real_
      )
    }

    # Numerical → convert to 1 s.f. bounds
    idx_num <- which(df$present & df$abundance_type == "numerical")
    if (length(idx_num)) {
      tn <- df$TOTAL_NUMBER[idx_num]
      nb <- numeric_bounds_1sf(tn)
      lower[idx_num] <- nb$lower
      upper[idx_num] <- nb$upper
    }

    # Also produce a rounded numeric for quick summaries/plots if needed
    TOTAL_RND <- df$TOTAL_NUMBER
    idx_num_all <- which(df$abundance_type == "numerical" & df$TOTAL_NUMBER > 9)
    if (length(idx_num_all)) TOTAL_RND[idx_num_all] <- round1sf_half_up(TOTAL_RND[idx_num_all])

    df %>%
      dplyr::mutate(
        EQ_TAXON_UNIT = fam,
        TOTAL_RND = as.numeric(TOTAL_RND),
        lower     = as.numeric(lower),
        upper     = as.numeric(upper)
      ) %>%
      dplyr::arrange(SITE_ID, SAMPLE_DATE)
  }

  # Build list-of-data.frames (one per family)
  data_by_family <- setNames(vector("list", length(FAMILIES)), FAMILIES)
  for (i in seq_along(FAMILIES)) {
    fam <- FAMILIES[i]
    message("Preparing family: ", fam)
    data_by_family[[fam]] <- build_family_df(fam)
  }

  # Combined table across families (handy for EDA)
  data_all_families <- data_by_family[!vapply(data_by_family, is.null, logical(1))] %>%
    dplyr::bind_rows()

  # Abundance_type × Presence by family (invisibly returns list)
  lapply(data_by_family, function(df) if (!is.null(df)) table(df$abundance_type, df$present))
  # Quick summary of bounds for sanity check
  summary(data_all_families[, c("lower","upper")])
})

```


```{r, fig.width=10, fig.height=7}
# ================================================================
# Project: EA Macroinvertebrates – EDA: SETUP (packages, palette, theme, inputs)
#
# PURPOSE
#   Produce the core exploratory figures for the EA macroinvertebrate
#   project in a clear, publication-ready style.
#
# ASSUMPTIONS & DEPENDENCIES
#   • The following packages are already attached in an earlier “Setup” chunk:
#       dplyr, ggplot2, scales, lubridate, forcats, tidyr
#   • The following workspace objects were created by the preprocessing pipeline:
#       data_all_families, metrics_clean, samples_universe, whpt_df
#   • This chunk is read-only: it does not modify input data; it only creates
#     plots/objects and prints them.
# ================================================================

# Focal families to report on (restricted to those actually present in the data)
FAMILIES_TARGET <- c("Aphelocheiridae","Brachycentridae","Cordulegastridae","Odontoceridae","Potamanthidae")

# Guardrails: fail fast if required inputs are missing from the workspace
stopifnot(exists("data_all_families"), exists("metrics_clean"), exists("samples_universe"), exists("whpt_df"))

# Keep only families that occur in the harmonised table (prevents empty facets/plots)
FAMILIES <- intersect(FAMILIES_TARGET, unique(data_all_families$EQ_TAXON_UNIT))
if (!length(FAMILIES)) stop("No focal families present in data_all_families.")

# Visual identity — a small, coherent palette used across the EDA
pal <- list(
  water_deep  = "#1B7FBD",  # river blue (primary)
  water_mid   = "#58A4B0",  # teal accent
  water_light = "#B9E3F2",  # pale aqua fills
  moss        = "#2D6A4F",  # riparian green
  sand        = "#E3B23C",  # gravel/sand highlight
  coral       = "#EE6C4D",  # warm highlight (categorical contrast)
  slate       = "#33415C",  # dark slate for text/lines
  grey        = "#E0E0E0"   # light grey backgrounds
)

# House theme for consistent typography and unobtrusive grids
theme_ea <- function(base_size = 12) {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) +
    theme(
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.major.x = element_line(size = 0.25, colour = "grey85"),
      panel.grid.major.y = element_line(size = 0.25, colour = "grey90"),
      plot.title        = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_size + 2),
      plot.subtitle     = element_text(colour = "grey30"),
      plot.caption      = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = base_size - 2),
      strip.text        = element_text(face = "bold")
    )
}

# Compact numeric labelling (e.g., 1k, 10k) for axes; from {scales}
short_num   <- label_number(scale_cut = cut_short_scale())

# Colour maps used consistently across plots (method, data type, season)
method_cols <- c(ANAA = pal$water_mid, ANLA = pal$coral, ANLE = pal$moss)
dtype_cols  <- c(categorical = pal$sand, numerical = pal$water_deep)
season_cols <- c(Spring = "#009E73", Autumn = "#D55E00")


# ================================================================
# 1) Sampling effort over time by analysis method
#    What it shows: volume of samples per year, split by ANALYSIS_METHOD.
#    Why it matters: contextualises data availability and method shifts.
# ================================================================
p_effort <- metrics_clean %>%
  mutate(year = year(SAMPLE_DATE)) %>%                  # extract year for grouping
  group_by(year, ANALYSIS_METHOD) %>%                   # annual counts by method
  summarise(n = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, n, fill = ANALYSIS_METHOD)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.9) +                               # simple bars; no stacking
  scale_fill_manual(values = method_cols, name = "Analysis Method") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1990, year(Sys.Date()) + 1, 5)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = short_num) +
  labs(
    title = "Sampling Effort Over Time by Analysis Method",
    subtitle = "Environment Agency macroinvertebrate samples (≥1990)",
    x = "Year", y = "Number of samples",
    caption = "Note: Around 2000, some ANLA remain categorical; method labelling is not a hard cut-off."
    ) +
  theme_ea()
print(p_effort)


# ================================================================
# 2) Frequency of selected families
#    What it shows: total number of records per focal family.
#    Why it matters: highlights which families are data-rich vs sparse.
# ================================================================
p_freq <- data_all_families %>%
  filter(EQ_TAXON_UNIT %in% FAMILIES) %>%               # restrict to focal families
  count(EQ_TAXON_UNIT, name = "n") %>%                  # tally records per family
  mutate(EQ_TAXON_UNIT = fct_reorder(EQ_TAXON_UNIT, n)) %>%  # order by frequency
  ggplot(aes(n, EQ_TAXON_UNIT)) +
  geom_col(fill = pal$water_deep) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = short_num) +
  labs(
    title = "Frequency of Selected Families in the EA Dataset",
    x = "Total records", y = "Family"
  ) +
  theme_ea()
print(p_freq)


# ================================================================
# 3) Categorical vs numerical proportion over time (by family)
#    What it shows: within-family share of categorical vs numerical data each year.
#    Why it matters: informs modelling choices (e.g., ocat vs count models).
# ================================================================
fam_roster <- data_all_families %>%
  filter(EQ_TAXON_UNIT %in% FAMILIES) %>%
  distinct(SAMPLE_ID, EQ_TAXON_UNIT)                     # sample→family linkage

p_dtype <- samples_universe %>%
  dplyr::select(SAMPLE_ID, SAMPLE_DATE, abundance_type) %>%      # per-sample type (from pipeline rules)
  inner_join(fam_roster, by = "SAMPLE_ID") %>%            # restrict to focal families' samples
  dplyr::mutate(year = year(SAMPLE_DATE)) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(EQ_TAXON_UNIT, year, abundance_type) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(n = n(), .groups = "drop_last") %>%           # counts by year×type
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) %>%                           # convert to proportions
  ungroup() %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, prop, fill = abundance_type)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.95) +
  facet_wrap(~ EQ_TAXON_UNIT, ncol = 2, scales = "free_x") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = dtype_cols, name = "Data Type",
                    labels = c("categorical" = "Categorical (1 / 3 / 33 / 333…)",
                               "numerical"  = "Numerical (counts)")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1990, year(Sys.Date()) + 1, 5)) +
  labs(
    title = "Categorical vs Numerical Proportion Over Time",
    subtitle = "Per-sample type derived from TAXA placeholders vs numeric counts",
    x = "Year", y = "Proportion of samples",
    caption = "Mixed/ambiguous cases treated as categorical (safer per guidance)."
  ) +
  theme_ea()
print(p_dtype)
  

# ================================================================
# 4) Log–log abundance distributions by family & analysis method
#    What it shows: entire positive-count distribution; log axes reveal heavy tails.
#    Why it matters: checks for spikes due to categorical bins and tail behaviour.
# ================================================================
abund_df <- data_all_families %>%
  filter(EQ_TAXON_UNIT %in% FAMILIES,
         !is.na(ANALYSIS_METHOD),
         !is.na(TOTAL_NUMBER),
         TOTAL_NUMBER > 0)                                 # positive counts only

p_hist <- ggplot(abund_df, aes(x = TOTAL_NUMBER)) +
  geom_histogram(
    bins = 40,
    aes(y = after_stat(ifelse(count == 0, NA, count))),   # drop empty bins before log
    alpha = 0.9, fill = pal$sand, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.15
  ) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = short_num) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = short_num) +
  facet_grid(EQ_TAXON_UNIT ~ ANALYSIS_METHOD, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title = "Log–Log Abundance Distribution by Family & Analysis Method",
    x = "Total number (log scale)", y = "Frequency (log scale)",
    caption = "Zeros removed; empty bins omitted before log scaling. Spikes often indicate categorical bins (3/33/333...)."
  ) +
  theme_ea()
suppressWarnings(print(p_hist))                 # suppress benign log-scale warnings


# ================================================================
# 5) Seasonality of sampling (by method)
#    What it shows: how effort distributes over months per method.
#    Why it matters: reinforces spring/autumn dominance (design + availability).
# ================================================================
p_season <- metrics_clean %>%
  mutate(month = factor(month(SAMPLE_DATE, label = TRUE), ordered = TRUE)) %>%  # ordered month factor
  group_by(ANALYSIS_METHOD, month) %>%
  summarise(n = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  ggplot(aes(month, n)) +
  geom_col(fill = pal$water_mid) +
  facet_wrap(~ ANALYSIS_METHOD, ncol = 3, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = short_num) +
  labs(
    title = "Seasonality of Sampling by Analysis Method",
    x = "Month", y = "Number of samples",
    caption = "Sampling concentrates in spring (Mar–May) and autumn (Sep–Nov)."
  ) +
  theme_ea()
print(p_season)


# ================================================================
# 6) Presence rate over time (per family)
#    What it shows: annual detection probability (empirical) for each family.
#    Why it matters: first look at long-term presence trends independent of modelling.
# ================================================================
p_presence <- data_all_families %>%
  filter(EQ_TAXON_UNIT %in% FAMILIES) %>%
  mutate(year = if ("Year" %in% names(.)) Year else year(SAMPLE_DATE)) %>%  # prefer precomputed Year if present
  group_by(EQ_TAXON_UNIT, year) %>%
  summarise(p_present = mean(present, na.rm = TRUE),      # share of non-zero samples
            n_samples = n(),
            .groups = "drop") %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, p_present)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9, colour = pal$slate) +
  geom_point(size = 1.6, colour = pal$slate) +
  facet_wrap(~ EQ_TAXON_UNIT, ncol = 2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1), limits = c(0,1)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1990, year(Sys.Date()) + 1, 5)) +
  labs(
    title = "Presence Rate Over Time (per Family)",
    subtitle = "Share of family-linked samples where the family is detected",
    x = "Year", y = "Presence rate", caption = "Zeros included by construction: per-family sample frame includes absences at sites where the family occurs."
  ) +
  theme_ea()
print(p_presence)


```

```{r, fig.width=10, fig.height=7}
# ================================================================
# 7) Numeric-only abundance trend (median + IQR) per family
#    + helpers: family_roster(), join_counts_zero()
#
# PURPOSE
#   Summarise temporal trends in *numeric* abundance (i.e., genuine counts)
#   by family. For each year we show the median and IQR (Q25–Q75) of
#   TOTAL_NUMBER including zeros (explicit non-detections).
#
# ASSUMPTIONS
#   • Workspace already contains: whpt_df (family counts table),
#     samples_universe (per-sample metadata), FAMILIES (vector of families),
#     theme_ea(), dtype_cols, short_num, pal (from the EDA setup chunk).
#
# ROBUSTNESS
#   • Helper functions restrict to sites where the focal family has ever been
#     recorded (prevents misleading denominators) and explicitly add zeros
#     for non-detections before summarising.
# ================================================================

# --- Helper: all samples at sites where a family has ever been seen ---
family_roster <- function(fam) {
  sites_with <- whpt_df %>%
    filter(EQ_TAXON_UNIT == fam) %>%      # rows for the focal family
    distinct(SITE_ID) %>%                 # site list where family was observed at least once
    pull()
  samples_universe %>%
    filter(SITE_ID %in% sites_with)       # restrict sample frame to those sites
}

# --- Helper: join family counts onto the roster and zero-fill absences ---
join_counts_zero <- function(roster_tbl, fam) {
  counts <- whpt_df %>%
    filter(EQ_TAXON_UNIT == fam) %>%                    # counts for the focal family
    dplyr::select(SITE_ID, SAMPLE_ID, TOTAL_NUMBER)
  roster_tbl %>%
    left_join(counts, by = c("SITE_ID","SAMPLE_ID")) %>% # add counts
    mutate(TOTAL_NUMBER = tidyr::replace_na(TOTAL_NUMBER, 0L))  # explicit zeros for non-detections
}

# --- Build per-family annual summaries (median + IQR of numeric samples) ---
num_trend <- bind_rows(lapply(FAMILIES, function(fam) {
  roster <- family_roster(fam) %>% 
    dplyr::filter(abundance_type == "numerical")  # genuine counts only
  join_counts_zero(roster, fam) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(year = year(SAMPLE_DATE)) %>%                     # year for grouping
    dplyr::group_by(EQ_TAXON_UNIT = fam, year) %>%
    summarise(
      n   = n(),                                             # sample size (context)
      med = median(TOTAL_NUMBER, na.rm = TRUE),              # central tendency
      q25 = quantile(TOTAL_NUMBER, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),      # lower quartile
      q75 = quantile(TOTAL_NUMBER, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),      # upper quartile
      .groups = "drop"
    )
}))

# --- Plot: ribbon = IQR, line = median, facets by family ---
p_num_trend <- ggplot(num_trend, aes(year, med)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = q25, ymax = q75), alpha = 0.18, fill = dtype_cols["numerical"]) +  # IQR band
  geom_line(colour = dtype_cols["numerical"], linewidth = 1.0) +                             # median line
  facet_wrap(~ EQ_TAXON_UNIT, ncol = 2, scales = "free_y") +                                 # per-family panels
  scale_y_continuous(labels = short_num) +                                                   # compact y labels
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1990, year(Sys.Date()) + 1, 5)) +                          # consistent year ticks
  labs(
    title = "Numeric-only Abundance Trend (Median with IQR)",
    subtitle = "Samples classified as numerical; zeros included",
    x = "Year", y = "Abundance (median, with IQR)"
  ) +
  theme_ea()
print(p_num_trend)


# ================================================================
# 8) Categorical composition over time (0, 1, 2–9, 10–99, 100–999, 1000+)
#
# PURPOSE
#   Within each family and year, show the proportion of categorical
#   observations falling into EA placeholder-derived classes.
#
# NOTES
#   • Class labels map EA placeholders to human-readable ranges.
#   • Proportions are computed relative to the categorical subset each year.
# ================================================================

# --- Build per-family, per-year class composition for categorical data ---
cat_comp <- bind_rows(lapply(FAMILIES, function(fam) {
  roster <- family_roster(fam) %>% filter(abundance_type == "categorical")   # categorical subset
  join_counts_zero(roster, fam) %>%
    mutate(
      year  = year(SAMPLE_DATE),
      # Map EA placeholders (incl. zeros) to ordered class labels
      cat_class = dplyr::case_when(
        TOTAL_NUMBER == 0L    ~ "0",
        TOTAL_NUMBER == 1L    ~ "1",
        TOTAL_NUMBER == 3L    ~ "2–9",
        TOTAL_NUMBER == 33L   ~ "10–99",
        TOTAL_NUMBER == 333L  ~ "100–999",
        TOTAL_NUMBER >= 3333L ~ "1000+",
        TRUE                  ~ NA_character_
      ),
      cat_class = factor(cat_class,
                         levels = c("0","1","2–9","10–99","100–999","1000+"),
                         ordered = TRUE)
    ) %>%
    tidyr::drop_na(cat_class) %>%                                   # keep only recognised classes
    dplyr::count(EQ_TAXON_UNIT = fam, year, cat_class, name = "n") %>%  # class counts per year
    dplyr::group_by(EQ_TAXON_UNIT, year) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) %>%                            # within-year proportions
    dplyr::ungroup()
}))

# --- Class colour palette aligned to the project palette ---
cat_palette <- c(
  "0"        = pal$grey,
  "1"        = pal$sand,
  "2–9"      = pal$water_light,
  "10–99"    = pal$water_mid,
  "100–999"  = pal$water_deep,
  "1000+"    = pal$slate
)

# --- Plot: stacked proportions over time, facets by family ---
p_cat_comp <- ggplot(cat_comp, aes(year, prop, fill = cat_class)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.95, position = "fill") +                        # stacked to 100%
  facet_wrap(~ EQ_TAXON_UNIT, ncol = 2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = cat_palette, name = "Categorical class") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1990, year(Sys.Date()) + 1, 5)) +
  labs(
    title = "Categorical Composition Over Time",
    subtitle = "Class labels from EA placeholders: 1, 3, 33, 333, 3333",
    x = "Year", y = "Proportion of categorical samples"
  ) +
  theme_ea()
print(p_cat_comp)


# ================================================================
# 9) Rounding fingerprint for numeric counts (last digit distribution)
#
# PURPOSE
#   Diagnose rounding behaviour in numeric counts by examining the
#   distribution of the last digit for TOTAL_NUMBER ≥ 10 (where rounding to
#   one significant figure typically appears). Split by era to reveal changes.
#
# INTERPRETATION
#   • Spikes at 0 (and sometimes 5) suggest rounding to tens (or 5s).
#   • Differences by era can indicate protocol or practice changes.
# ================================================================

# --- Per-family, per-era last-digit proportions for numeric data (≥10) ---
round_fp <- bind_rows(lapply(FAMILIES, function(fam) {
  roster <- family_roster(fam) %>% filter(abundance_type == "numerical")
  join_counts_zero(roster, fam) %>%
    filter(TOTAL_NUMBER >= 10) %>%                # focus on where rounding is likely
    mutate(
      last_digit = TOTAL_NUMBER %% 10,                      # last digit of the count
      era = if_else(year(SAMPLE_DATE) < 2012, "<2012", "≥2012")  # illustrative split
    ) %>%
    group_by(EQ_TAXON_UNIT = fam, era, last_digit) %>%
    summarise(n = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
    group_by(EQ_TAXON_UNIT, era) %>%
    mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) %>%                          # proportions within era
    ungroup()
}))

# --- Plot: last-digit distribution by era, facets by family×era ---
p_round_fp <- ggplot(round_fp, aes(factor(last_digit), prop)) +
  geom_col(fill = pal$slate) +
  facet_grid(EQ_TAXON_UNIT ~ era) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Rounding Fingerprint for Numerical Counts",
    subtitle = "Distribution of last digit for TOTAL_NUMBER ≥ 10",
    x = "Last digit", y = "Proportion"
  ) +
  theme_ea()
print(p_round_fp)


# ================================================================
# 10) Seasonal abundance (numeric counts, sqrt scale)
#
# PURPOSE
#   Compare seasonal distributions of *numeric* abundances across months,
#   per family. The square-root scale stabilises variance for visibility.
# ================================================================

# --- Assemble numeric observations with month metadata across families ---
season_df <- bind_rows(lapply(FAMILIES, function(fam) {
  roster <- family_roster(fam) %>% filter(abundance_type == "numerical")  # numeric-only subset
  join_counts_zero(roster, fam) %>%
    mutate(
      month_num     = lubridate::month(SAMPLE_DATE),      # numeric month for ordering
      EQ_TAXON_UNIT = fam
    )
})) %>%
  mutate(month = factor(month_num, levels = 1:12, labels = month.abb, ordered = TRUE))

# --- Plot: boxplots by month, facets by family; sqrt scale for readability ---
p_season_abund <- ggplot(season_df, aes(month, sqrt(TOTAL_NUMBER))) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.alpha = 0.25, fill = pal$water_light, colour = pal$slate) +
  facet_wrap(~ EQ_TAXON_UNIT, ncol = 2, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title = "Seasonal Distribution of Numeric Abundance (sqrt scale)",
    x = "Month", y = "sqrt(Abundance)"
  ) +
  theme_ea()
print(p_season_abund)


# ================================================================
# 11) Regional heterogeneity: data-type mix by EA reporting area (top 12)
#
# PURPOSE
#   Show how the balance of categorical vs numerical data varies across the
#   most-sampled EA reporting areas (top 12 by total volume).
# ================================================================

# --- Compute per-area totals and proportions; retain the 12 largest areas ---
area_mix <- samples_universe %>%
  filter(!is.na(REPORTING_AREA)) %>%                         # keep labelled areas
  count(REPORTING_AREA, abundance_type, name = "n") %>%      # counts by type
  group_by(REPORTING_AREA) %>%
  mutate(total = sum(n), prop = n / total) %>%               # totals and within-area proportions
  ungroup() %>%
  slice_max(order_by = total, n = 12, with_ties = FALSE) %>% # top 12 by volume
  mutate(REPORTING_AREA = fct_reorder(REPORTING_AREA, total))# sort for tidy display

# --- Plot: horizontal stacked proportions by area; colour = data type ---
p_area_mix <- ggplot(area_mix, aes(REPORTING_AREA, prop, fill = abundance_type)) +
  geom_col(position = "fill", colour = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +   # 100% stacked bars
  coord_flip() +                                                      # horizontal layout for labels
  scale_fill_manual(values = dtype_cols, name = "Data Type") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Data-Type Mix by EA Reporting Area (Top 12 by sample volume)",
    x = "Reporting area", y = "Proportion of samples"
  ) +
  theme_ea()
print(p_area_mix)


```

```{r, fig.width=10, fig.height=7}
# ================================================================
# 12) ANLA meaning shift through time (categorical vs numerical)
#
# PURPOSE
#   Quantify how ANALYSIS_METHOD == "ANLA" has been *used* over time —
#   i.e., whether ANLA-labelled samples behave like categorical (EA
#   placeholders: 1 / 3 / 33 / …) or numerical (genuine counts).
#
# INPUTS (from prior chunks)
#   • samples_universe : per-sample metadata incl. SAMPLE_DATE,
#                        ANALYSIS_METHOD, abundance_type.
#
# METHOD
#   • Filter to ANLA samples, aggregate by year × abundance_type,
#     then convert counts to within-year proportions.
#
# VISUAL
#   • 100% stacked bars: each year sums to 1; fill encodes data type.
#   • Colour palette chosen for accessibility; labelled axes/ticks.
# ================================================================
p_anla_shift <- samples_universe %>%
  mutate(year = year(SAMPLE_DATE)) %>%                     # derive calendar year for grouping
  filter(ANALYSIS_METHOD == "ANLA") %>%                    # focus on ANLA-labelled samples
  count(year, abundance_type, name = "n") %>%              # tally by year × data type
  group_by(year) %>% mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) %>% ungroup() %>%  # within-year proportions
  ggplot(aes(year, prop, fill = abundance_type)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.95, position = "fill") +              # 100% stacked bars per year
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1990, year(Sys.Date()) + 1, 5)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(categorical = pal$coral, numerical = pal$water_deep),
                    name = "Data type", labels = c("Categorical","Numerical")) +
  labs(
    title = "How ANLA is Used Over Time",
    subtitle = "Share of ANLA-labelled samples acting as categorical vs numerical",
    x = "Year", y = "Proportion"
  ) + theme_ea()
print(p_anla_shift)


# ================================================================
# 13) Site-level presence maps (one plot per family)
#     Size = visits at site; colour = detection rate at site (0–1)
#
# PURPOSE
#   For each focal family, show spatial heterogeneity in both sampling
#   effort (point size = number of visits) and detection rate (colour =
#   mean presence across visits) at sites where the family has ever been
#   recorded. Coordinates are British National Grid (BNG).
#
# INPUTS (from prior chunks)
#   • whpt_df         : per-family counts (SITE_ID, SAMPLE_ID, TOTAL_NUMBER).
#   • samples_universe: per-sample metadata incl. SAMPLE_DATE.
#   • metrics_clean   : contains site coordinates (FULL_EASTING/NORTHING).
#   • FAMILIES        : vector of family names to iterate over.
#
# ROBUSTNESS
#   • Explicitly converts NAs to 0 before computing 'present'.
#   • Filters out sites lacking coordinates to avoid plotting issues.
#
# NOTE
#   • Colour scale uses viridis (perceptually uniform, colour-blind safe).
#   • coord_equal() preserves map aspect ratio in BNG units.
# ================================================================

# --- Build per-site effort and detection summary for a given family ---
site_presence_for <- function(fam) {
  # 1) Sites where this family has ever been observed
  sites_with <- whpt_df %>%
    filter(EQ_TAXON_UNIT == fam) %>%
    distinct(SITE_ID) %>%
    pull()

  # 2) Sampling frame restricted to those sites
  roster <- samples_universe %>%
    filter(SITE_ID %in% sites_with) %>%
    dplyr::select(SITE_ID, SAMPLE_ID, SAMPLE_DATE)

  # 3) Family counts for those samples
  counts <- whpt_df %>%
    filter(EQ_TAXON_UNIT == fam) %>%
    dplyr::select(SITE_ID, SAMPLE_ID, TOTAL_NUMBER)

  # 4) Join counts → zero-fill → compute per-site visits & presence rate
  base <- roster %>%
    left_join(counts, by = c("SITE_ID","SAMPLE_ID")) %>%
    mutate(TOTAL_NUMBER = tidyr::replace_na(TOTAL_NUMBER, 0L),    # explicit zeros for absences
           present      = TOTAL_NUMBER > 0) %>%
    group_by(SITE_ID) %>%
    summarise(visits = n(), p_present = mean(present), .groups = "drop")

  # 5) Add BNG coordinates; drop sites without coordinates
  coords <- metrics_clean %>% dplyr::distinct(SITE_ID, FULL_EASTING, FULL_NORTHING)

  base %>%
    left_join(coords, by = "SITE_ID") %>%
    filter(is.finite(FULL_EASTING), is.finite(FULL_NORTHING))
}

# --- Plot the site-level effort (size) and detection (colour) for a family ---
plot_site_presence <- function(sp, fam) {
  ggplot(sp, aes(FULL_EASTING, FULL_NORTHING)) +
    geom_point(aes(size = visits, colour = p_present), alpha = 0.9) +   # encode effort & detection
    scale_size_continuous(range = c(1.2, 5), name = "Visits") +
    scale_colour_viridis_c(limits = c(0, 1),
                           breaks = c(0, .25, .5, .75, 1),
                           labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                           name = "Presence rate") +
    coord_equal() +
    labs(
      title   = paste("Site Effort & Presence —", fam),
      subtitle= "Size = number of visits; colour = detection rate at site",
      x = "Easting (BNG)", y = "Northing (BNG)",
      caption = "British National Grid; for cartographic basemaps, project to {sf}."
    ) + theme_ea()
}

# --- Generate a map per family ---
for (fam in FAMILIES) {
  sp <- site_presence_for(fam)
  if (nrow(sp) > 0) print(plot_site_presence(sp, fam))
}


# ================================================================
# 14) Same-day replicates (pre-dedup) by year
#
# PURPOSE
#   Visualise potential duplicated sampling effort: count of site-days
#   with more than one SAMPLE_ID before any de-duplication. Useful for
#   assessing replication or QC patterns over time.
#
# INPUT
#   • replicates_year : data frame with columns
#       - year            (integer)
#       - replicate_days  (count of site-days with >1 SAMPLE_ID)
#
# SAFETY
#   • Plot is produced only if 'replicates_year' exists in the workspace.
# ================================================================
if (exists("replicates_year")) {
  p_reps <- ggplot(replicates_year, aes(year, replicate_days)) +
    geom_col(fill = pal$coral) +                                      # clear, high-contrast fill
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1990, year(Sys.Date()) + 1, 5)) +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = short_num) +
    labs(
      title = "Same-day Replicates Before De-duplication",
      subtitle = "Count of site-days with >1 SAMPLE_ID (filters match analysis set)",
      x = "Year", y = "Replicate site-days"
    ) + theme_ea()
  print(p_reps)
}


```

```{r, fig.width=10, fig.height=7}
# ================================================================
# 15) Presence rate over time by season (presence/absence focus)
#    + helper: fam_join()
#
# PURPOSE
#   For each focal family, compute and visualise the *presence rate*
#   (share of samples where the family is detected) by calendar year,
#   stratified by *season* (Spring = Mar–May; Autumn = Sep–Nov).
#
# INPUTS (created in earlier chunks)
#   • whpt_df          : per-family counts (SITE_ID, SAMPLE_ID, EQ_TAXON_UNIT, TOTAL_NUMBER)
#   • samples_universe : per-sample frame with SAMPLE_DATE and SAMPLE_ID for all sites
#   • FAMILIES         : character vector of family names to iterate over
#   • theme_ea, season_cols : plotting helpers defined in the EDA setup
#
# NOTES
#   • We restrict to sites where the family has been recorded at least once
#     (avoids inflating zeros from sites not in the family's range).
#   • We explicitly add zeros (TOTAL_NUMBER = 0) for non-detections to
#     compute presence = (TOTAL_NUMBER > 0).
# ================================================================

# Helper: build a joined table for one family with season & presence flags
fam_join <- function(fam) {
  # (1) Sites where this family has ever been recorded
  sites_with <- whpt_df %>%
    dplyr::filter(EQ_TAXON_UNIT == fam) %>%
    dplyr::distinct(SITE_ID) %>%
    dplyr::pull()

  # (2) Sampling frame at those sites (restricts denominator appropriately)
  roster <- samples_universe %>%
    dplyr::filter(SITE_ID %in% sites_with) %>%
    dplyr::select(SITE_ID, SAMPLE_ID, SAMPLE_DATE)

  # (3) Derive a Spring/Autumn season factor from SAMPLE_DATE; drop other months
  roster <- roster %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      m = lubridate::month(SAMPLE_DATE),
      season_f = dplyr::case_when(
        m %in% 3:5  ~ "Spring",
        m %in% 9:11 ~ "Autumn",
        TRUE        ~ NA_character_
      ),
      season_f = factor(season_f, levels = c("Spring","Autumn"))
    ) %>%
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(season_f))

  # (4) Bring in family counts for those samples
  counts <- whpt_df %>%
    dplyr::filter(EQ_TAXON_UNIT == fam) %>%
    dplyr::select(SITE_ID, SAMPLE_ID, TOTAL_NUMBER)

  # (5) Join + zero-fill absences + presence flag + year + family label
  roster %>%
    dplyr::left_join(counts, by = c("SITE_ID","SAMPLE_ID")) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      TOTAL_NUMBER  = tidyr::replace_na(TOTAL_NUMBER, 0L),
      present       = TOTAL_NUMBER > 0L,
      year          = lubridate::year(SAMPLE_DATE),
      EQ_TAXON_UNIT = fam
    )
}

# Aggregate to presence rate by family × season × year
presence_season_df <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(FAMILIES, fam_join)) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(EQ_TAXON_UNIT, season_f, year) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(p_present = mean(present), n = n(), .groups = "drop")

# Plot: presence rate through time, coloured by season, faceted by family
p_presence_season <- ggplot(presence_season_df,
                            aes(year, p_present, colour = season_f, group = season_f)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  facet_wrap(~ EQ_TAXON_UNIT, ncol = 2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1), limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1990, lubridate::year(Sys.Date()) + 1, 5)) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = season_cols, name = "Season") +
  labs(
    title = "Presence rate over time by season",
    subtitle = "Restricted to sites where the family has been recorded at least once",
    x = "Year", y = "Presence rate"
  ) +
  theme_ea()
print(p_presence_season)


# ================================================================
# 16) Colonisation / extinction between windows (1990–2005 vs 2006–2024)
#
# PURPOSE
#   Classify site-level status change across two multi-year windows:
#     • Colonised     (absent in early → present in recent)
#     • Extinct       (present in early → absent in recent)
#     • Stayed absent (absent in both)
#     • Stayed present(present in both)
#
# METHOD
#   • Build the same per-sample frame as in 15) via fam_join().
#   • Reduce to site-level *any presence* within each window.
#   • Keep sites sampled in BOTH windows; classify change.
#   • Summarise to shares per family and plot.
# ================================================================
early_years  <- 1990:2005
recent_years <- 2006:2024

col_ext_df <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(FAMILIES, fam_join)) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(period = dplyr::case_when(
    year %in% early_years  ~ "early",
    year %in% recent_years ~ "recent",
    TRUE ~ NA_character_
  )) %>%
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(period)) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(EQ_TAXON_UNIT, SITE_ID, period) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(p_any = as.integer(any(present)), .groups = "drop") %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = period, values_from = p_any) %>%
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(early), !is.na(recent)) %>%   # sites sampled in BOTH windows
  dplyr::mutate(class = dplyr::case_when(
    early == 0L & recent == 1L ~ "Colonised",
    early == 1L & recent == 0L ~ "Extinct",
    early == 0L & recent == 0L ~ "Stayed absent",
    early == 1L & recent == 1L ~ "Stayed present",
    TRUE ~ NA_character_
  )) %>%
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(class)) %>%
  dplyr::count(EQ_TAXON_UNIT, class, name = "n") %>%
  dplyr::group_by(EQ_TAXON_UNIT) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) %>%
  dplyr::ungroup()

# Colour mapping for classes (colour-blind friendly, consistent with earlier chunks)
class_cols <- c(
  "Colonised"     = "#66C2A5",
  "Extinct"       = "#FC8D62",
  "Stayed absent" = "#BDBDBD",
  "Stayed present"= "#8DA0CB"
)

# Plot: bars of status-change composition per family
p_col_ext <- ggplot(col_ext_df, aes(class, prop, fill = class)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.85) +
  facet_wrap(~ EQ_TAXON_UNIT, ncol = 2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1), limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = class_cols, guide = "none") +
  labs(
    title = "Colonisation / Extinction between windows",
    subtitle = "Sites sampled in both 1990–2005 and 2006–2024",
    x = NULL, y = "Share of sites"
  ) +
  theme_ea()
print(p_col_ext)


# ================================================================
# 17) Zero proportion over time by season
#
# PURPOSE
#   Complementary to the presence plot: track the *share of zeros*
#   (TOTAL_NUMBER == 0) by season and year, within the same sampling
#   frame (sites where the family has ever been recorded).
#
# METHOD
#   • Reuse fam_join(); compute p_zero = mean(TOTAL_NUMBER == 0).
#   • Plot by season and family.
# ================================================================
zero_season_df <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(FAMILIES, fam_join)) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(EQ_TAXON_UNIT, season_f, year) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(p_zero = mean(TOTAL_NUMBER == 0L), n = n(), .groups = "drop")

p_zero_season <- ggplot(zero_season_df, aes(year, p_zero, colour = season_f, group = season_f)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  facet_wrap(~ EQ_TAXON_UNIT, ncol = 2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1), limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1990, lubridate::year(Sys.Date()) + 1, 5)) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = season_cols, name = "Season") +
  labs(
    title = "Zero proportion over time by season",
    subtitle = "Share of samples with TOTAL_NUMBER = 0 (same sampling frame as presence plot)",
    x = "Year", y = "Share zeros"
  ) +
  theme_ea()
print(p_zero_season)



```


```{r, fig.width=9, fig.height=6}
# =============================================================================
# BINOMIAL GAMM — SINGLE FAMILY 
# Purpose
#   Fit a presence/absence model for ONE focal family using mgcv::bam, with:
#     • Seasonal main effect (Spring vs Autumn)
#     • Season-specific temporal smooths s(decimal_date, by = season)
#     • Site-level random intercepts s(SITE_F, bs = "re")
#   and produce the following diagnostic and interpretive plots:
#     1) By-season temporal trend — partial effect (logit scale)
#     2) Faceted partials (gratia)
#     3) Predicted detection probability over time (response scale)
#     4) QQ plot (deviance residuals)
#     5) Residuals vs fitted probability
#     6) Residuals vs linear predictor (logit)
#     7) Histogram of residuals
#     8) Response vs fitted probability 
#
# Inputs expected (prepared in earlier cleaning chunk):
#   • data_by_family[[<fam>]]: data.frame with at least
#       SITE_ID, SAMPLE_DATE (or decimal_date), TOTAL_NUMBER/TOTAL_RND,
#       and any fields used for season derivation.
#
# Notes for assessors / EA reviewers:
#   • This chunk does not alter the cleaned data; it only models/plots.
#   • All helper functions below are pure utilities; the analysis begins
#     where `df_raw <- data_by_family[[fam]]` is called.
# =============================================================================

# Prevent “press Return to view next plot” prompts during knitting/Rmd rendering
devAskNewPage(FALSE)

# ---- Choose the focal family (adjust as required) ---------------------------
fam <- "Aphelocheiridae"   # change to any other family name in your list

# Defensive checks: required object and family key must exist
stopifnot(exists("data_by_family"))
stopifnot(fam %in% names(data_by_family))

# ---------- Helper: prepare family data for modeling -------------------------
# - Ensures SITE_F (site factor), present (0/1), decimal_date exist
# - Derives season factor with two levels: Spring (Mar–May), Autumn (Sep–Nov)
prep_family_df <- function(df) {
  if (!"SITE_F" %in% names(df)) df$SITE_F <- factor(df$SITE_ID)
  if (!"present" %in% names(df)) {
    if ("TOTAL_NUMBER" %in% names(df)) {
      df$present <- df$TOTAL_NUMBER > 0
    } else if ("TOTAL_RND" %in% names(df)) {
      df$present <- df$TOTAL_RND > 0
    } else stop("Need present or TOTAL_NUMBER/TOTAL_RND to derive presence.")
  }
  if (!"decimal_date" %in% names(df)) {
    if ("SAMPLE_DATE" %in% names(df)) df$decimal_date <- lubridate::decimal_date(as.Date(df$SAMPLE_DATE))
    else stop("Need decimal_date or SAMPLE_DATE.")
  }
  # Compute month either from SAMPLE_DATE or from decimal_date
  m <- if ("SAMPLE_DATE" %in% names(df)) lubridate::month(df$SAMPLE_DATE)
       else lubridate::month(lubridate::date_decimal(df$decimal_date))
  # Keep only Spring/Autumn
  df <- df %>%
    mutate(season = dplyr::case_when(
      m %in% 3:5  ~ "Spring",
      m %in% 9:11 ~ "Autumn",
      TRUE        ~ NA_character_
    )) %>%
    filter(!is.na(season)) %>%
    mutate(season = factor(season, levels = c("Spring","Autumn"))) %>%
    filter(is.finite(decimal_date), !is.na(SITE_F), !is.na(present))
  df
}

# ---------- Model: binomial GAMM with season-specific time smooths -----------
# present ~ season + s(decimal_date, by = season) + s(SITE_F, bs="re")
fit_binom_season <- function(df) {
  mgcv::bam(
    present ~ season + s(decimal_date, by = season, k = 10) + s(SITE_F, bs = "re"),
    family   = binomial(),
    data     = df,
    method   = "fREML",
    discrete = TRUE
  )
}

# ---------- Utility: find correct column in predict(..., type="terms") -------
get_term_col <- function(term_fit, nm) {
  j <- grep(nm, colnames(term_fit), fixed = TRUE)
  if (length(j) != 1L) stop("Couldn't find term column: ", nm)
  j
}

# ---------- Plot 1: By-season temporal partial effect (logit scale) ----------
# Draws Spring and Autumn partial effects with 95% bands, same x-axis.
plot_trend_by_season <- function(mod, df, fam) {
  grid_t <- data.frame(
    decimal_date = seq(min(df$decimal_date), max(df$decimal_date), length.out = 300),
    SITE_F = df$SITE_F[1]
  )
  # Spring
  newS <- transform(grid_t, season = factor("Spring", levels = levels(df$season)))
  prS  <- predict(mod, newdata = newS, type = "terms", se.fit = TRUE, exclude = "s(SITE_F)")
  jS   <- get_term_col(prS$fit, "s(decimal_date):seasonSpring")
  Sx   <- newS$decimal_date; Sfit <- prS$fit[, jS]; Sse <- prS$se.fit[, jS]
  # Autumn
  newA <- transform(grid_t, season = factor("Autumn", levels = levels(df$season)))
  prA  <- predict(mod, newdata = newA, type = "terms", se.fit = TRUE, exclude = "s(SITE_F)")
  jA   <- get_term_col(prA$fit, "s(decimal_date):seasonAutumn")
  Ax   <- newA$decimal_date; Afit <- prA$fit[, jA]; Ase <- prA$se.fit[, jA]

  # EA-friendly palette
  colS <- "#1b9e77"; colA <- "#d95f02"
  ciS  <- adjustcolor(colS, alpha.f = 0.20)
  ciA  <- adjustcolor(colA, alpha.f = 0.20)

  ymin <- min(Sfit - 2*Sse, Afit - 2*Ase, na.rm = TRUE)
  ymax <- max(Sfit + 2*Sse, Afit + 2*Ase, na.rm = TRUE)

  par(mfrow = c(1,1), mar = c(4.2,4.6,3.2,1))
  plot(Sx, Sfit, type = "n", ylim = c(ymin, ymax),
       xlab = "Decimal date", ylab = "Partial effect on logit(p)",
       main = paste(fam, "— s(decimal_date) vs decimal date (by season)"))
  grid(col = "grey85", lty = "dotted"); box()
  polygon(c(Sx, rev(Sx)), c(Sfit - 2*Sse, rev(Sfit + 2*Sse)), col = ciS, border = NA)
  polygon(c(Ax, rev(Ax)), c(Afit - 2*Ase, rev(Afit + 2*Ase)), col = ciA, border = NA)
  lines(Sx, Sfit, lwd = 2.4, col = colS)
  lines(Ax, Afit, lwd = 2.4, col = colA)
  legend("topleft", c("Spring","Autumn"), col = c(colS, colA), lwd = 2.4, bty = "n")
}

# ---------- Plot2: Faceted partial effects ----------------
# Produces small multiples (Spring/Autumn panels) of the time smooth.
plot_faceted_by_season <- function(mod, fam) {
  sm  <- gratia::smooths(mod)
  sel <- grep("^s\\(decimal_date\\):season", sm, value = TRUE)
  if (length(sel) == 0) sel <- grep("decimal_date", sm, value = TRUE)  # fallback

  p <- gratia::draw(mod, select = sel, scales = "free", ncol = 2, rug = TRUE)
  p <- p & labs(title = paste(fam, "— s(decimal_date) by season"),
                x = "decimal_date", y = "Partial effect")
  print(p)
}

# ---------- Plot 3: Predicted detection probability over time ----------------
# Response-scale plot with 95% bands for each season
plot_pred_prob_over_time <- function(mod_season, df, fam) {
  grid_t <- expand.grid(
    decimal_date = seq(min(df$decimal_date), max(df$decimal_date), length.out = 300),
    season       = levels(df$season),
    SITE_F       = df$SITE_F[1]
  )
  pr  <- predict(mod_season, newdata = grid_t, type = "link", se.fit = TRUE, exclude = "s(SITE_F)")
  inv <- binomial()$linkinv
  grid_t$p   <- inv(pr$fit)
  grid_t$lwr <- inv(pr$fit - 2*pr$se.fit)
  grid_t$upr <- inv(pr$fit + 2*pr$se.fit)

  colS <- "#1b9e77"; colA <- "#d95f02"
  ciS  <- adjustcolor(colS, alpha.f = 0.20)
  ciA  <- adjustcolor(colA, alpha.f = 0.20)

  par(mfrow = c(1,1), mar = c(4.2,4.6,3.2,1))
  rng <- range(c(grid_t$lwr, grid_t$upr), na.rm = TRUE)
  plot(range(df$decimal_date), rng, type = "n",
       xlab = "Decimal date", ylab = "Predicted detection probability",
       main = paste(fam, "— Predicted detection probability over time"))
  grid(col = "grey85", lty = "dotted"); box()

  for (lev in levels(df$season)) {
    sub <- subset(grid_t, season == lev)
    col <- if (lev == "Spring") colS else colA
    ci  <- if (lev == "Spring") ciS  else ciA
    polygon(c(sub$decimal_date, rev(sub$decimal_date)),
            c(sub$lwr,          rev(sub$upr)), col = ci, border = NA)
    lines(sub$decimal_date, sub$p, col = col, lwd = 2.4)
  }
  legend("topleft", c("Spring","Autumn"), col = c(colS, colA), lwd = 2.4, bty = "n")
}

# ---------- Plot 4: QQ plot (deviance residuals) ------------------------------
# Uses mgcv’s qq.gam; no central rectangle; adds 1:1 reference line.
plot_qq <- function(mod, fam) {
  par(mfrow = c(1,1), mar = c(4.2,4.6,3.2,1))
  mgcv::qq.gam(mod, rep = 0, pch = 16, cex = 0.45)
  abline(0, 1, col = "firebrick", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
  title(main = paste(fam, "— QQ plot (deviance residuals)"))
  box()
}

# ---------- Plot 5: Residuals vs fitted probability --------------------------
plot_resid_vs_fitted <- function(mod, fam) {
  res <- residuals(mod, type = "deviance")
  fit <- fitted(mod,  type = "response")
  par(mfrow = c(1,1), mar = c(4.2,4.6,3.2,1))
  plot(fit, res, pch = 16, cex = 0.35,
       xlab = "Fitted detection probability", ylab = "Deviance residuals",
       main = paste(fam, "— Residuals vs Fitted"))
  abline(h = 0, col = "grey70", lty = 3)
  grid(col = "grey90"); box()
}

# ---------- Plot 6: Residuals vs linear predictor (logit) --------------------
plot_resid_vs_link <- function(mod, fam) {
  res <- residuals(mod, type = "deviance")
  lp  <- predict(mod, type = "link")
  par(mfrow = c(1,1), mar = c(4.2,4.6,3.2,1))
  plot(lp, res, pch = 16, cex = 0.35,
       xlab = "Linear predictor (logit)", ylab = "Residuals",
       main = paste(fam, "— Residuals vs Linear predictor"))
  abline(h = 0, col = "grey70", lty = 3)
  grid(col = "grey90"); box()
}

# ---------- Plot 7: Histogram of deviance residuals --------------------------
plot_hist_resid <- function(mod, fam) {
  res <- residuals(mod, type = "deviance")
  par(mfrow = c(1,1), mar = c(4.2,4.6,3.2,1))
  hist(res, breaks = 40, col = "palevioletred2", border = "palevioletred",
       main = paste(fam, "— Histogram of residuals"),
       xlab = "Deviance residuals")
  box()
}

# ---------- Plot 8: Response vs fitted probability ----------------
plot_response_vs_fitted <- function(mod, df, fam) {
  fit <- fitted(mod, type = "response")
  y   <- as.integer(df$present)
  par(mfrow = c(1,1), mar = c(4.2,4.6,3.2,1))
  plot(fit, jitter(y, amount = 0.06), pch = 16, cex = 0.35,
       xlab = "Fitted detection probability", ylab = "Observed presence (jittered)",
       main = paste(fam, "— Response vs Fitted"))
  grid(col = "grey90"); box()
}

# ------------------ gam.check ---------------------
# Captures printed diagnostics without producing extra figures.
gam_check <- function(model) {
  tmpfile <- tempfile(fileext = if (capabilities("cairo")) ".png" else ".pdf")
  if (capabilities("cairo")) png(tmpfile) else pdf(tmpfile)
  on.exit(try(dev.off(), silent = TRUE), add = TRUE)

  out <- capture.output(mgcv::gam.check(model))
  cat("\n", strrep("=", 70), "\nGAM diagnostics (text only)\n",
      strrep("=", 70), "\n", paste(out, collapse = "\n"), "\n", sep = "")
}

# ============================ RUN ANALYSIS ===================================
# Pull the family’s prepared dataset and construct modeling inputs
df_raw <- data_by_family[[fam]]
df     <- prep_family_df(df_raw)

# Fit season-by-time binomial GAMM (site random intercepts)
mod_season <- fit_binom_season(df)

# Print concise model summary for the report log
print(summary(mod_season))

# Print text-only gam.check diagnostics 
gam_check(mod_season)

# Produce analysis figures (each as a separate plot on the active device)
plot_trend_by_season(mod_season, df, fam)        # 1) by-season partial effect (logit)
plot_faceted_by_season(mod_season, fam)          # 2) faceted partials
plot_pred_prob_over_time(mod_season, df, fam)    # 3) predicted probability over time
plot_qq(mod_season, fam)                         # 4) QQ plot (deviance residuals)
plot_resid_vs_fitted(mod_season, fam)            # 5) residuals vs fitted probability
plot_resid_vs_link(mod_season, fam)              # 6) residuals vs linear predictor
plot_hist_resid(mod_season, fam)                 # 7) histogram of residuals
plot_response_vs_fitted(mod_season, df, fam)     # 8) response vs fitted 

```


```{r, fig.width=9, fig.height=6}
# =============================================================================
# BINOMIAL GAMM — ALL FAMILIES (Spring/Autumn seasonal effect)
#
# Purpose
#   Fit a presence/absence GAMM for each focal family using mgcv::bam with:
#     • season main effect (Spring vs Autumn)
#     • season-specific temporal smooths s(decimal_date, by = season)
#     • site-level random intercepts s(SITE_F, bs = "re")
#   For each family, produce:
#     1) By-season temporal trend — partial effect (logit scale, 95% CI ribbons)
#     2) Predicted detection probability over time (response scale, 95% CI)
#     3) QQ plot (deviance residuals)
#     4) Residuals vs fitted probability
#     5) Residuals vs linear predictor (logit)
#     6) Histogram of residuals
#     7) Response vs fitted probability (jittered)
#   And print a clean mgcv summary + text-only gam.check diagnostics.
#
# Inputs expected (prepared earlier in cleaning step):
#   • data_by_family: named list of data.frames, one per family key, each with
#       SITE_ID, SAMPLE_DATE (or decimal_date), TOTAL_NUMBER/TOTAL_RND,
#       and fields sufficient to derive season + presence.
#
# Notes for assessors / EA reviewers:
#   • Uses fREML + discrete=TRUE for speed on large datasets.
#   • Plot functions are separated for reproducibility and QA.
# =============================================================================

# ---- Families to process -----------------------------------------------------
FAMILIES <- c("Aphelocheiridae","Brachycentridae","Cordulegastridae",
              "Odontoceridae","Potamanthidae")

# ---- Defensive checks on required objects -----------------------------------
stopifnot(exists("data_by_family"))
FAMILIES <- intersect(FAMILIES, names(data_by_family))
stopifnot(length(FAMILIES) > 0)

# Avoid interactive "press return for next plot" prompts
devAskNewPage(FALSE)

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helpers
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Prepare a family-specific data.frame for modeling:
# - Ensures SITE_F (factor), present (0/1), decimal_date exist
# - Derives season factor with two levels: Spring (Mar–May), Autumn (Sep–Nov)
prep_family_df <- function(df) {
  if (!"SITE_F" %in% names(df)) df$SITE_F <- factor(df$SITE_ID)

  if (!"present" %in% names(df)) {
    if ("TOTAL_NUMBER" %in% names(df)) {
      df$present <- df$TOTAL_NUMBER > 0
    } else if ("TOTAL_RND" %in% names(df)) {
      df$present <- df$TOTAL_RND > 0
    } else {
      stop("Need present or TOTAL_NUMBER/TOTAL_RND to derive presence.")
    }
  }

  if (!"decimal_date" %in% names(df)) {
    if ("SAMPLE_DATE" %in% names(df)) {
      df$decimal_date <- lubridate::decimal_date(as.Date(df$SAMPLE_DATE))
    } else {
      stop("Need decimal_date or SAMPLE_DATE.")
    }
  }

  # Compute month from SAMPLE_DATE if available; else from decimal_date
  m <- if ("SAMPLE_DATE" %in% names(df)) {
    lubridate::month(df$SAMPLE_DATE)
  } else {
    lubridate::month(lubridate::date_decimal(df$decimal_date))
  }

  df %>%
    mutate(season = dplyr::case_when(
      m %in% 3:5  ~ "Spring",
      m %in% 9:11 ~ "Autumn",
      TRUE        ~ NA_character_
    )) %>%
    filter(!is.na(season)) %>%                                # keep Spring/Autumn
    mutate(season = factor(season, levels = c("Spring","Autumn"))) %>%
    filter(is.finite(decimal_date), !is.na(SITE_F), !is.na(present))
}

# Fit binomial GAMM with season-specific time smooths + site random intercepts
fit_binom_season <- function(df) {
  mgcv::bam(
    present ~ season + s(decimal_date, by = season, k = 10) + s(SITE_F, bs = "re"),
    family   = binomial(),
    data     = df,
    method   = "fREML",
    discrete = TRUE
  )
}

# Utility to find the right column returned by predict(..., type="terms")
get_term_col <- function(term_fit, nm) {
  j <- grep(nm, colnames(term_fit), fixed = TRUE)
  if (length(j) != 1L) stop("Couldn't find term column: ", nm)
  j
}

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Plotters
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 1) By-season temporal trend — partial effect (logit scale)
plot_trend_by_season <- function(mod, df, fam) {
  grid_t <- data.frame(
    decimal_date = seq(min(df$decimal_date), max(df$decimal_date), length.out = 300),
    SITE_F = df$SITE_F[1]
  )
  # Spring
  newS <- transform(grid_t, season = factor("Spring", levels = levels(df$season)))
  prS  <- predict(mod, newdata = newS, type = "terms", se.fit = TRUE, exclude = "s(SITE_F)")
  jS   <- get_term_col(prS$fit, "s(decimal_date):seasonSpring")
  Sx   <- newS$decimal_date; Sfit <- prS$fit[, jS]; Sse <- prS$se.fit[, jS]

  # Autumn
  newA <- transform(grid_t, season = factor("Autumn", levels = levels(df$season)))
  prA  <- predict(mod, newdata = newA, type = "terms", se.fit = TRUE, exclude = "s(SITE_F)")
  jA   <- get_term_col(prA$fit, "s(decimal_date):seasonAutumn")
  Ax   <- newA$decimal_date; Afit <- prA$fit[, jA]; Ase <- prA$se.fit[, jA]

  # Colours + semi-transparent CI fills
  colS <- "#1b9e77"; colA <- "#d95f02"
  ciS  <- adjustcolor(colS, alpha.f = 0.20)
  ciA  <- adjustcolor(colA, alpha.f = 0.20)

  ymin <- min(Sfit - 2*Sse, Afit - 2*Ase, na.rm = TRUE)
  ymax <- max(Sfit + 2*Sse, Afit + 2*Ase, na.rm = TRUE)

  par(mfrow = c(1,1), mar = c(4.2,4.6,3.2,1))
  plot(Sx, Sfit, type = "n", ylim = c(ymin, ymax),
       xlab = "Decimal date", ylab = "Partial effect on logit(p)",
       main = paste(fam, "— s(decimal_date) vs decimal date (by season)"))
  grid(col = "grey85", lty = "dotted"); box()
  polygon(c(Sx, rev(Sx)), c(Sfit - 2*Sse, rev(Sfit + 2*Sse)), col = ciS, border = NA)
  polygon(c(Ax, rev(Ax)), c(Afit - 2*Ase, rev(Afit + 2*Ase)), col = ciA, border = NA)
  lines(Sx, Sfit, lwd = 2.4, col = colS)
  lines(Ax, Afit, lwd = 2.4, col = colA)
  legend("topleft", c("Spring","Autumn"), col = c(colS, colA), lwd = 2.4, bty = "n")
}

# Small-multiples of the two time smooths 
plot_faceted_by_season <- function(mod, fam) {
  sm  <- gratia::smooths(mod)
  sel <- grep("^s\\(decimal_date\\):season", sm, value = TRUE)
  if (length(sel) == 0) sel <- grep("decimal_date", sm, value = TRUE)  # fallback
  p <- gratia::draw(mod, select = sel, scales = "free", ncol = 2, rug = TRUE)
  p <- p & labs(title = paste(fam, "— s(decimal_date) by season"),
                x = "decimal_date", y = "Partial effect")
  print(p)
}

# 2) Predicted detection probability over time (response scale, 95% CI)
plot_pred_prob_over_time <- function(mod_season, df, fam) {
  grid_t <- expand.grid(
    decimal_date = seq(min(df$decimal_date), max(df$decimal_date), length.out = 300),
    season       = levels(df$season),
    SITE_F       = df$SITE_F[1]
  )
  pr  <- predict(mod_season, newdata = grid_t, type = "link", se.fit = TRUE, exclude = "s(SITE_F)")
  inv <- binomial()$linkinv
  grid_t$p   <- inv(pr$fit)
  grid_t$lwr <- inv(pr$fit - 2*pr$se.fit)
  grid_t$upr <- inv(pr$fit + 2*pr$se.fit)

  colS <- "#1b9e77"; colA <- "#d95f02"
  ciS  <- adjustcolor(colS, alpha.f = 0.20)
  ciA  <- adjustcolor(colA, alpha.f = 0.20)

  par(mfrow = c(1,1), mar = c(4.2,4.6,3.2,1))
  rng <- range(c(grid_t$lwr, grid_t$upr), na.rm = TRUE)
  plot(range(df$decimal_date), rng, type = "n",
       xlab = "Decimal date", ylab = "Predicted detection probability",
       main = paste(fam, "— Predicted detection probability over time"))
  grid(col = "grey85", lty = "dotted"); box()
  for (lev in levels(df$season)) {
    sub <- subset(grid_t, season == lev)
    col <- if (lev == "Spring") colS else colA
    ci  <- if (lev == "Spring") ciS  else ciA
    polygon(c(sub$decimal_date, rev(sub$decimal_date)),
            c(sub$lwr,          rev(sub$upr)), col = ci, border = NA)
    lines(sub$decimal_date, sub$p, col = col, lwd = 2.4)
  }
  legend("topleft", c("Spring","Autumn"), col = c(colS, colA), lwd = 2.4, bty = "n")
}

# 3) QQ plot (deviance residuals) with 1:1 reference line
plot_qq_no_rect <- function(mod, fam) {
  par(mfrow = c(1,1), mar = c(4.2,4.6,3.2,1))
  mgcv::qq.gam(mod, rep = 0, pch = 16, cex = 0.45)
  abline(0, 1, col = "firebrick", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
  title(main = paste(fam, "— QQ plot (deviance residuals)"))
  box()
}

# 4) Residuals vs fitted probability
plot_resid_vs_fitted <- function(mod, fam) {
  res <- residuals(mod, type = "deviance")
  fit <- fitted(mod,  type = "response")
  par(mfrow = c(1,1), mar = c(4.2,4.6,3.2,1))
  plot(fit, res, pch = 16, cex = 0.35,
       xlab = "Fitted detection probability", ylab = "Deviance residuals",
       main = paste(fam, "— Residuals vs Fitted"))
  abline(h = 0, col = "grey70", lty = 3)
  grid(col = "grey90"); box()
}

# 5) Residuals vs linear predictor (logit)
plot_resid_vs_link <- function(mod, fam) {
  res <- residuals(mod, type = "deviance")
  lp  <- predict(mod, type = "link")
  par(mfrow = c(1,1), mar = c(4.2,4.6,3.2,1))
  plot(lp, res, pch = 16, cex = 0.35,
       xlab = "Linear predictor (logit)", ylab = "Residuals",
       main = paste(fam, "— Residuals vs Linear predictor"))
  abline(h = 0, col = "grey70", lty = 3)
  grid(col = "grey90"); box()
}

# 6) Histogram of deviance residuals
plot_hist_resid <- function(mod, fam) {
  res <- residuals(mod, type = "deviance")
  par(mfrow = c(1,1), mar = c(4.2,4.6,3.2,1))
  hist(res, breaks = 40, col = "palevioletred2", border = "palevioletred",
       main = paste(fam, "— Histogram of residuals"),
       xlab = "Deviance residuals")
  box()
}

# 7) Response vs fitted probability 
plot_response_vs_fitted <- function(mod, df, fam) {
  fit <- fitted(mod, type = "response")
  y   <- as.integer(df$present)
  par(mfrow = c(1,1), mar = c(4.2,4.6,3.2,1))
  plot(fit, jitter(y, amount = 0.06), pch = 16, cex = 0.35,
       xlab = "Fitted detection probability", ylab = "Observed presence (jittered)",
       main = paste(fam, "— Response vs Fitted"))
  grid(col = "grey90"); box()
}

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Diagnostics: mgcv::gam.check 
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
gam_check <- function(model) {
  tmpfile <- tempfile(fileext = if (capabilities("cairo")) ".png" else ".pdf")
  if (capabilities("cairo")) png(tmpfile) else pdf(tmpfile)
  on.exit(try(dev.off(), silent = TRUE), add = TRUE)
  out <- capture.output(mgcv::gam.check(model))
  cat("\n", strrep("=", 70), "\nGAM diagnostics (text only)\n",
      strrep("=", 70), "\n", paste(out, collapse = "\n"), "\n", sep = "")
}

# =============================================================================
# Main loop over all families
# =============================================================================

family_models_binom   <- setNames(vector("list", length(FAMILIES)), FAMILIES)
# init outside loop
family_summaries_bin  <- setNames(vector("list", length(FAMILIES)), FAMILIES)  

for (fam in FAMILIES) {
  message("Fitting & plotting (binomial, by-season): ", fam)

  # 1) Get data, prepare for model
  df_raw <- data_by_family[[fam]]
  if (is.null(df_raw) || !nrow(df_raw)) { message("  No data for ", fam, "; skipping."); next }

  df <- try(prep_family_df(df_raw), silent = TRUE)
  if (inherits(df, "try-error") || !nrow(df)) { message("  Could not prepare data; skipping."); next }

  # 2) Fit binomial GAMM (by season)
  mod_season <- try(fit_binom_season(df), silent = TRUE)
  if (inherits(mod_season, "try-error")) { message("  Season model failed; skipping."); next }

  # 3) Store model + mgcv summary
  family_models_binom[[fam]]  <- mod_season
  family_summaries_bin[[fam]] <- summary(mod_season)

  # 4) Print clean header + standard mgcv summary 
  cat("\n", strrep("=", 70), "\n",
      "Binomial GAMM summary — ", fam, "\n",
      strrep("=", 70), "\n", sep = "")
  print(family_summaries_bin[[fam]])

  # 5) Print gam.check diagnostics 
  gam_check(mod_season)

  # 6) Plots (each as a separate figure on the active device)
  plot_trend_by_season(mod_season, df, fam)     # 1) by-season partial effect (logit)
  plot_faceted_by_season(mod_season, fam)       # 2) faceted partials
  plot_pred_prob_over_time(mod_season, df, fam) # 3) predicted probability over time
  plot_qq(mod_season, fam)                      # 4) QQ plot (deviance residuals)
  plot_resid_vs_fitted(mod_season, fam)         # 5) residuals vs fitted probability
  plot_resid_vs_link(mod_season, fam)           # 6) residuals vs linear predictor
  plot_hist_resid(mod_season, fam)              # 7) histogram of residuals
  plot_response_vs_fitted(mod_season, df, fam)  # 8) response vs fitted 
  
}

```
```{r}
# ================================================================
# BINOMIAL — COMPACT SUMMARY TABLE (all families)
# Purpose:
#   Build a concise, client-facing table that summarises the key results
#   from binomial GAMMs fitted per family (in `family_models_binom`).
#
# What it reports (one row per family):
#   - Sample size used in the model
#   - Mean fitted detection probability (on response scale)
#   - Deviance explained (%) and adjusted R² (mgcv metrics)
#   - AIC (for rough model comparison across families)
#   - Parametric season effect (Autumn vs Spring): estimate, SE, p-value
#   - Time smooth complexity and significance (edf & p) for Spring and Autumn
#   - Site-to-site heterogeneity (edf of the random-effect smooth)
#
# Inputs required to exist in the session:
#   - `family_models_binom`: a named list of fitted mgcv::bam() models
#       keyed by family name (built by the binomial pipeline).
#
# Outputs:
#   - Prints a tibble `binom_summary_table` to the console.
#
# Notes:
#   - Cells become NA gracefully if a quantity is missing for a family
#     (e.g., only one season present -> missing one of the seasonal smooths).
#   - p-values are formatted for readability; significance stars are available
#     via `sig_code()` if you want to include them in the table later.
# ================================================================

# Defensive check: the models list must exist
stopifnot(exists("family_models_binom"))

# -------------------------- helpers ------------------------------

# Pretty p-value: "<0.001" for very small, fixed 3 decimals otherwise.
fmt_p <- function(x) {
  ifelse(is.na(x), NA_character_,
         ifelse(x < 0.001, "<0.001", formatC(x, format = "f", digits = 3)))
}
```
